agent1_snippets.title: Zidovudine -- Toxicity
agent1_snippets.content: A patient who ingested 10 to 20 grams of zidovudine in addition to phenobarbital and triazolam 8 hours prior to presentation complained of headache and nausea. His vitals were stable, but a physical exam was significant for ocular nystagmus and upper and lower extremity ataxia. Hemoglobin was stable at 14.8 g/dl. He was given intravenous fluids and his nystagmus and ataxia resolved within 48 hours. At six weeks follow-up, his hemoglobin was 17.0 g/dl. [22]

agent1_snippets.title: HIV-1–Associated Progressive Polyradiculopathy -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: In HIV patients with CMV-PP, intravenous ganciclovir administration for 2 to 3 weeks is the first-line treatment for severe disease, while oral valganciclovir could be given for mild disease. Maintainance therapy with oral valganciclovir is advised for 6 months or until the CD4 count is above 0.1 x 10 9 /L. [40] If the patient gives a history of previous monotherapy for similar or different CMV manifestations or if the serial CSF reading shows persistent pleocytosis and hypoglycorrhachia after induction of ganciclovir therapy, the likelihood of treatment failure is high, and treatment with alternative drug or ganciclovir-foscarnet combination should be considered. In ganciclovir resistance, foscarnet infusion can be given alone or in combination with cidofovir.

agent1_snippets.title: Lamivudine -- Administration -- Special Patient Populations
agent1_snippets.content: Hepatic impairment: No dose adjustment is necessary in patients with hepatic impairment. Renal impairment: Doses should be adjusted accordingly for the patient's kidney function: Creatinine clearance greater than or equal to 50 mL/min should have a lamivudine dose of 150 mg twice per day or 300 mg once per day. Creatinine clearance of 30 to 49 mL/min should have a lamivudine dose of 150 mg once per day. If a patient's creatinine clearance is between 15 to 29 mL/min, they should receive 150 mg as a first dose, then 100 mg daily afterward. Creatinine clearance of 5 to 14 mL/min equates to 150 mg as a first dose, then 50 mg daily afterward. With a creatinine clearance of less than 5 mL/min, the patient would receive 50 mg as the first dose and then 25 mg per day. Additional dosing is not necessary after either 4-hour routine hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis.

agent1_snippets.title: Labyrinthitis -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Viral labyrinthitis treatment typically involves hydration and bed rest in an outpatient setting. However, patients must be counseled to seek further medical intervention if their condition worsens or they develop neurological symptoms (e.g., weakness/numbness, diplopia, slurred speech, and gait disturbance). [45] Notably, antiviral medications and steroids are not supported by current evidence. [46] [47]

agent1_snippets.title: Tabes Dorsalis -- Treatment / Management -- Posttreatment Surveillance
agent1_snippets.content: Following treatment, patients should undergo a neurologic examination and lumbar puncture at 3 and 6 months, followed by repeat evaluations at 6-month intervals until the CSF white blood cell count is normal and the CSF-VDRL is nonreactive. [3] Successful treatment is marked by a decline in the CSF white blood cell count within 6 months, with complete resolution of all CSF abnormalities within 2 years after treatment. Retreatment is necessary if the patient experiences any increase in the CSF white blood cell count, a 4-fold increase in CSF-VDRL titer, or if the CSF white blood cell count does not decrease within 6 months, or if all CSF abnormalities do not resolve within 2 years. CSF abnormalities may normalize slower in patients with HIV infection.

agent1_snippets.title: HIV-1–Associated Progressive Polyradiculopathy -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: With prompt therapy, the progression of neurological symptoms is controlled within 2 weeks, and virological tests become negative in a 3-week course. However, residual neurological deficits are still commonly seen after treatment completion. The antivirals prevent further virus replication; however, they do not cure the latent infection; thus, relapses are frequently seen. Hence, many studies suggest an indefinite course of ganciclovir in these patients. [18] [28] [41] Foscarnet is a valuable alternative drug for acyclovir-resistant herpes virus infection. [10]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1574
agent2_snippets.content: Antipsychotics are linked to خلل الحركة المتأخر, a late‑onset movement disorder (commonly known as tardive dyskinesia) that can develop after prolonged use of these medications; recognizing this relationship is essential for clinicians to monitor patients on antipsychotic therapy for abnormal involuntary movements and to adjust treatment strategies to reduce the risk of this adverse effect.
agent2_snippets.query_used:
A 65-year old man presents with gradually worsening rigidity of his arms and legs and slowness in performing tasks. He says he has also noticed hand tremors, which increase at rest and decrease with focused movements. On examination, the patient does not swing his arms while walking and has a shortened, shuffling gait. An antiviral drug is prescribed which alleviates the patient’s symptoms. Which of the following drugs was most likely prescribed to this patient?

Options:
A: Amantadine
B: Ribavirin
C: Levodopa
D: Zidovudine

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1129
agent2_snippets.content: Protease inhibitors, classified as direct‑acting antivirals, constitute a therapeutic drug class that includes ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents act by specifically inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and reducing viral replication. As key components of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection, each of these drugs—ritonavir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir—shares the common mechanism of direct protease inhibition, making them integral to combination regimens aimed at achieving viral suppression and improving patient outcomes.

He says he has also noticed hand tremors, which increase at rest and decrease with focused movements. B: Ribavirin
C: Levodopa
D: Zidovudine

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 0 -- Community 7
agent2_snippets.content: Direct‑acting antivirals (DAAs) constitute a broad pharmacological class organized by chemical structure and viral target and encompass multiple subclasses such as Cyclic amines (e.g., rimantadine), Integrase inhibitors (e.g., cabotegravir, dolutegravir, raltegravir), Neuraminidase inhibitors (e.g., zanamivir), Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs; e.g., etravirine, delavirdine, delavirdine‑mesylate, efavirenz, nevirapine, rilpivirine), Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs; e.g., abacavir, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, tenofovir disoproxil, dideoxycytidine [zalcitabine]), and “Nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors” (e.g., ganciclovir, idoxuridin, cidofovir). Additional DAA groups include “Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections” (ribavirin, telaprevir), “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations” (fixed‑dose combinations abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and
